sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 65, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 64 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

Still not completed. I now have unique matches:

- B[49] → top 33

- B[53] → top 6

- Top section: 3 rows × 4 cols of colored tiles with symbols
- Bottom section: 2 rows × 5 cols of colored tiles with symbols
- There are white cursor blocks below the bottom tiles
Wait - maybe the goal is about the ORDER of symbols. Perhaps the bottom tiles need to be arranged to spell out or replicate a specific pattern from the top. Or maybe the bottom row needs to match the top row in some way.
Let me think about this differently. The top grid has:
Row 0: S, P, S, P (4 tiles)
Row 1: S, P, S, P (4 tiles)
Row 2: S, P, S, P (4 tiles)
The bottom has:
Row 0: S, S, S, S, S (5 tiles, all sky blue)
Row 1: P, P, P, P, P (5 tiles, all pink)
So the bottom has a different structure - all tiles in each row are the same color. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom tiles match specific top tiles based on some positional mapping.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to match each BOTTOM tile to the corresponding TOP tile by column. The top has 4 columns, the bottom has 5 columns. But maybe only the first 4 columns matter?
Top column 0: S tiles (ids 5, 17, 30)
Top column 1: P tiles (ids 6, 18, 31)
Top column 2: S tiles (ids 7, 19, 32)
Top column 3: P tiles (ids 8, 20, 33)
Bottom column 0: S (B[42]), P (B[49])
Bottom column 1: S (B[43]), P (B[50])
Bottom column 2: S (B[44]), P (B[51])
Bottom column 3: S (B[45]), P (B[52])
Bottom column 4: S (B[46]), P (B[53])
If the mapping is by column:
- Bottom col 0 should match top col 0 (S tiles) or top col 1 (P tiles)?
- B[42] (S) should match one of {5, 17, 30}
- B[49] (P) should match one of {6, 18, 31}
But B[42] = a5a03333dcefae48, which doesn't match any top tile. And B[42] can't be changed (cycle len=1).
Hmm, let me think about this more. Maybe the goal is to match bottom tiles to top tiles by ROW instead of column. Or maybe there's a different pattern.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the initial state again:
- B[53] (col 4 P) initially matched top 6 (which is top row, col 1 P)
- B[43] (col 1 S) always matches top 30 (which is bottom row, col 0 S)
These don't follow a simple column or row mapping.
Let me think about another possibility: maybe the goal is to make ALL bottom tiles match SOME top tile, and the ones that can't be changed (cycle len=1) are already in the correct state (or don't need to match).
Currently:
- B[42] (S, col 0): a5a03333dcefae48 - no match, can't change
- B[43] (S, col 1): 598e9cbf8e60024f - matches top 30, can't change
- B[44] (S, col 2): 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 - no match, can't change
- B[45] (S, col 3): bb9796c6c98e1001 - no match, can't change
- B[46] (S, col 4): 8e4cb671a12a4302 - no match, can't change
- B[49] (P, col 0): b4edb3faf810133b - matches top 33, can change
- B[50] (P, col 1): fb80c9b281b063da - matches top 18, can change
- B[51] (P, col 2): 5fcaa599a8d90976 - no match, can't change
- B[52] (P, col 3): f4469c244897540c - matches top 20, can't change
- B[53] (P, col 4): cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 - matches top 6, can't change
So 5 out of 10 bottom tiles match top tiles. The S tiles mostly don't match (except B[43]), and B[51] (col 2 P) doesn't match.
Maybe the goal requires ALL 10 bottom tiles to match? But the S tiles can't be changed, and most don't match. Unless... the S tiles are supposed to match top P tiles or vice versa?
Let me check if any S bottom tile hashes match top P hashes:
- B[42]: a5a03333dcefae48 vs top P {cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, d38ad0a046a963a0, fb80c9b281b063da, f4469c244897540c, 12ee96cc93cd3e0a, b4edb3faf810133b} - no match
- B[43]: 598e9cbf8e60024f - no match with top P
- B[44]: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 - no match
- B[45]: bb9796c6c98e1001 - no match
- B[46]: 8e4cb671a12a4302 - no match
And P bottom tiles matching top S hashes:
- B[49]: b4edb3faf810133b - no match with top S
- B[50]: fb80c9b281b063da - no match with top S
- B[51]: 5fcaa599a8d90976 - no match
- B[52]: f4469c244897540c - no match with top S
- B[53]: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 - no match with top S
So cross-color matching doesn't work either.
I'm stuck. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is not about matching symbols but about something else entirely. Let me look at the board structure more carefully.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the problem. Maybe I need to look at what the top tiles represent. The top grid has symbols on colored backgrounds. Maybe the symbols themselves have meaning, and I need to arrange the bottom tiles to spell something or create a pattern.
Alternatively, maybe the goal is to make the bottom row match a specific row from the top. For example, maybe the bottom P row should match the top row's P tiles in order.

- B[50] should be hash of top 8: d38ad0a046a963a0
- B[51] should be hash of top 18: fb80c9b281b063da

- B[53] should be hash of top 31: 12ee96cc93cd3e0a
But B[52] can't change (it's already f4469c244897540c = top 20, which matches position 4). And B[53] can't change (it's cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 = top 6, not top 31).
Hmm, that doesn't work either. Let me try another ordering.
Actually, let me look at the top grid layout more carefully:

Row 1: S[17], P[18], S[19], P[20]
Row 2: S[30], P[31], S[32], P[33]
So the P tiles in reading order are: 6, 8, 18, 20, 31, 33
And the S tiles in reading order are: 5, 7, 17, 19, 30, 32

If bottom P should match top P tiles 6, 8, 18, 20, 31 (first 5):
- B[49] = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (top 6)
- B[50] = d38ad0a046a963a0 (top 8)
- B[51] = fb80c9b281b063da (top 18) - but B[51] can't change!
- B[52] = f4469c244897540c (top 20) - already matches!
- B[53] = 12ee96cc93cd3e0a (top 31) - but B[53] can't change!
This doesn't work because B[51] and B[53] can't be changed to the required values.
Let me try yet another approach. Maybe the bottom tiles should match the LAST 5 top tiles of each color:
Top P (last 5): 8, 18, 20, 31, 33
- B[49] = d38ad0a046a963a0 (top 8)
- B[50] = fb80c9b281b063da (top 18) - currently matches!
- B[51] = f4469c244897540c (top 20) - but B[51] can't change!
- B[52] = 12ee96cc93cd3e0a (top 31) - but B[52] can't change!
- B[53] = b4edb3faf810133b (top 33) - but B[53] can't change!

I'm running out of hypotheses. Let me try something different - maybe I should look at the INITIAL state more carefully. In the initial state:
- B[49] = a2774f59f95f9997 (no match)
- B[50] = c66a83c4e7c00e20 (no match)
- B[51] = 5fcaa599a8d90976 (no match)
- B[52] = 8b72057039a2be38 (no match)
- B[53] = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (matches top 6)
Only B[53] matched initially. Maybe the goal is to match ALL bottom P tiles to ALL top P tiles in some order, and the ones that can't be changed are already correct?

B[52] = can't change, currently f4469c244897540c = top 20
B[51] = can't change, 5fcaa599a8d90976 (no match)
If B[51] can't match any top tile, then the goal can't be to match all tiles.
I'm really stuck now. Let me try a different approach - maybe I should look at what happens when I press certain actions. Maybe there's a "submit" or "check" action that I haven't discovered. But the valid actions are only UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Wait - maybe DOWN does something different from UP? I've only been using UP to cycle. Let me check if DOWN cycles in the opposite direction or does something else.
Also, maybe the goal involves the cursor position. Perhaps I need to position the cursor in a specific way to complete the level.
Let me try pressing DOWN to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try pressing DOWN to see if it does something different
# Also let me check if there's a pattern with the cursor positions

# First, let me check current cursor position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"Cursor: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try pressing DOWN
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter DOWN:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"Cursor: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check if any bottom hashes changed
print("\nBottom hashes after DOWN:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and 42 <= n['id'] <= 53:
        print(f"  B[{n['id']}]: {n['hash']}")

print(f"\nResult: {last_action_result}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
